taskOutput:
Done.

What I deployed
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns) with image nginx:1.29, 2 replicas, labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app=recommendation-engine (removed the version selector) so it sends traffic to both v2.0 and v2.1 pods.

Traffic split
- With engine-v2-0 at 2 replicas and engine-v2-1 at 2 replicas, the Service will distribute roughly 50% of connections to each version.

How to verify
- Check pods and labels: kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get pods -l app=recommendation-engine -o wide
- Check Service endpoints: kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns get endpoints recommendation-engine

Rollback options
- Quickest: scale canary to 0: kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns scale deploy/engine-v2-1 --replicas=0
- Or revert Service to only version v2.0: set selector to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0
- Or delete the canary deployment.

If you’d like an exact weighted split or per-request weighting (not just per-pod), we can switch to an ingress/service-mesh based canary with explicit weights.

What I deployed
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns) with image nginx:1.29, 2 replicas, labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1.
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to select app=recommendation-engine (removed the version selector) so it sends traffic to both v2.0 and v2.1 pods.

Traffic split
- With engine-v2-0 at 2 replicas and engine-v2-1 at 2 replicas, the Service will distribute roughly 50% of connections to each version.

- Egress to any pods in namespace ns2 (all ports)

All other egress is denied. Ingress is not affected.

  name: np
  namespace: ns1

  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:

      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53

taskOutput:
Short answer: The pod is healthy; the application code is intentionally throwing errors.

What I checked
- Pod status: Running and Ready; no warning/error events in calc-app namespace.
- Logs: intermittent “division by zero” errors.
- ConfigMap calc-app-map (mounted at /etc/config) contains this logic:
  - 25% of iterations hit: result = 1 / 0 (caught and logged as “division by zero”).
  - Sleep time grows exponentially: sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20, so logs slow down over time.

Why you see errors
- The line result = 1 / 0 inside the try block is executed when random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0 (i.e., ~25% of runs), so it will always raise ZeroDivisionError and print a failure message. This is by design in the current script, not a Kubernetes issue.

How to fix (if you don’t want failures)
- Change the script in the calc-app-map ConfigMap to avoid dividing by zero. For example:
  - Replace the failing branch with a safe path:
    if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
        print(f"Run {counter} skipped")
    else:
        denom = random.randint(1, 4)
        result = 1 / denom
        print(f"Run {counter} succeeded: {result}")
- Consider reducing or capping the exponential backoff if you want steady log output.

Deploying the fix
- Update the ConfigMap (kubectl -n calc-app edit configmap calc-app-map or apply a new YAML).
- Restart the pod so Python re-reads the script:
  - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod
- Tip: Use a Deployment instead of a naked Pod, and trigger rollouts on ConfigMap changes (e.g., by annotating a hash) for easier updates.
taskJudgeReason: Pass under CONTAINS: The model response explicitly identifies and explains that the issue stems from a division by zero in the application (e.g., logs show “division by zero,” code path result = 1 / 0 causing ZeroDivisionError). This fully contains the core information from the reference answer. Extra details provided are consistent and do not contradict the core meaning.

apiVersion: v1
data:
  calc-app.py: |
    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

Short answer: The pod is healthy; the application code is intentionally throwing errors.

What I checked
- Pod status: Running and Ready; no warning/error events in calc-app namespace.
- Logs: intermittent “division by zero” errors.
- ConfigMap calc-app-map (mounted at /etc/config) contains this logic:
  - 25% of iterations hit: result = 1 / 0 (caught and logged as “division by zero”).
  - Sleep time grows exponentially: sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20, so logs slow down over time.

Why you see errors
- The line result = 1 / 0 inside the try block is executed when random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0 (i.e., ~25% of runs), so it will always raise ZeroDivisionError and print a failure message. This is by design in the current script, not a Kubernetes issue.

How to fix (if you don’t want failures)
- Change the script in the calc-app-map ConfigMap to avoid dividing by zero. For example:
  - Replace the failing branch with a safe path:
    if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
        print(f"Run {counter} skipped")
    else:
        denom = random.randint(1, 4)
        result = 1 / denom
        print(f"Run {counter} succeeded: {result}")
- Consider reducing or capping the exponential backoff if you want steady log output.

Deploying the fix
- Update the ConfigMap (kubectl -n calc-app edit configmap calc-app-map or apply a new YAML).
- Restart the pod so Python re-reads the script:
  - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod
- Tip: Use a Deployment instead of a naked Pod, and trigger rollouts on ConfigMap changes (e.g., by annotating a hash) for easier updates.

taskOutput:
Fixed.

- Root cause: The container in Deployment crashloop-test/app was set to run /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which doesn’t exist, causing CrashLoopBackOff.
- Change made: Patched the Deployment to remove the custom command/args so it uses nginx’s default entrypoint.
- Current state:
  - Pod app-66686b6766-6bg7v: 1/1 Running
  - Deployment app: Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1

If you intended to run a custom command, share the correct command and I’ll update the Deployment accordingly.

Steps.message:
namespace/crashloop-test created
deployment.apps/app created

Fixed.

- Root cause: The container in Deployment crashloop-test/app was set to run /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, which doesn’t exist, causing CrashLoopBackOff.
- Change made: Patched the Deployment to remove the custom command/args so it uses nginx’s default entrypoint.
- Current state:
  - Pod app-66686b6766-6bg7v: 1/1 Running
  - Deployment app: Available=True, ReadyReplicas=1

If you intended to run a custom command, share the correct command and I’ll update the Deployment accordingly.

taskOutput:
I found the root cause: your pod mounts PVC homepage-pvc, which referenced a non-existent StorageClass sc. That kept the PVC unbound, so the pod stayed Pending.

What I did
- Created a StorageClass named sc using the cluster’s local-path provisioner (same as your default “standard” class).
- The PVC was then provisioned and bound, and the pod scheduled and started.

Current status
- Pod homepage-pod in homepage-ns: Running and Ready (1/1)
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (provisioned by rancher.io/local-path)

If you prefer not to keep the extra StorageClass “sc,” we can instead recreate the PVC to use the existing “standard” StorageClass. That would require deleting/recreating the PVC (and handling data retention carefully). Want me to switch it over?

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

I found the root cause: your pod mounts PVC homepage-pvc, which referenced a non-existent StorageClass sc. That kept the PVC unbound, so the pod stayed Pending.

What I did
- Created a StorageClass named sc using the cluster’s local-path provisioner (same as your default “standard” class).
- The PVC was then provisioned and bound, and the pod scheduled and started.

Current status
- Pod homepage-pod in homepage-ns: Running and Ready (1/1)
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (provisioned by rancher.io/local-path)

If you prefer not to keep the extra StorageClass “sc,” we can instead recreate the PVC to use the existing “standard” StorageClass. That would require deleting/recreating the PVC (and handling data retention carefully). Want me to switch it over?

taskOutput:
I found the issue and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used nginx:latest with health probes pointing to /is_ready and /get_status. The stock nginx image doesn’t serve those paths, so the probes returned 404.
- As a result, the pod stayed unready and the Service had no healthy endpoints, so the app wasn’t reachable.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment’s readiness and liveness probes to GET / on port 80 and adjusted probe timings.
- Kubernetes rolled out a new pod and removed the old one.

Current status
- Pod webapp-68bd44946b-nrml7 is Ready.
- Service webapp now has endpoint 10.244.0.29:80.
- Probes are returning HTTP 200.

How to verify quickly
- kubectl -n orders get pods
- kubectl -n orders get endpoints webapp
- kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and browse http://localhost:8080

Optional next steps
- Do you want this webapp accessible externally? I can create an Ingress or switch the Service to NodePort/LoadBalancer.
- Prefer pinning nginx to a fixed version (e.g., nginx:1.29.5) instead of latest for stability. Want me to update it?

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found the issue and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used nginx:latest with health probes pointing to /is_ready and /get_status. The stock nginx image doesn’t serve those paths, so the probes returned 404.
- As a result, the pod stayed unready and the Service had no healthy endpoints, so the app wasn’t reachable.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment’s readiness and liveness probes to GET / on port 80 and adjusted probe timings.
- Kubernetes rolled out a new pod and removed the old one.

Current status
- Pod webapp-68bd44946b-nrml7 is Ready.
- Service webapp now has endpoint 10.244.0.29:80.
- Probes are returning HTTP 200.

How to verify quickly
- kubectl -n orders get pods
- kubectl -n orders get endpoints webapp
- kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and browse http://localhost:8080

Optional next steps
- Do you want this webapp accessible externally? I can create an Ingress or switch the Service to NodePort/LoadBalancer.
- Prefer pinning nginx to a fixed version (e.g., nginx:1.29.5) instead of latest for stability. Want me to update it?

taskOutput:
Root cause
- Your Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing apps/deployments, not core/v1 pods. The RoleBinding correctly bound the ServiceAccount, but the Role didn’t grant the needed permissions to pods.

What I fixed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to grant get, list, watch on core/v1 pods.

New Role spec
- apiGroups: [""]
- resources: ["pods"]
- verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i list pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

Notes
- If you need to list pods across all namespaces, use a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding instead.
- If you also want to read pod logs, add resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] with get, list, watch.

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role

Steps.message:
namespace/simple-rbac-setup created
serviceaccount/pod-reader created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-role created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-binding created

Root cause
- Your Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing apps/deployments, not core/v1 pods. The RoleBinding correctly bound the ServiceAccount, but the Role didn’t grant the needed permissions to pods.

What I fixed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to grant get, list, watch on core/v1 pods.

New Role spec
- apiGroups: [""]
- resources: ["pods"]
- verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i list pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

Notes
- If you need to list pods across all namespaces, use a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding instead.
- If you also want to read pod logs, add resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] with get, list, watch.

What I fixed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to grant get, list, watch on core/v1 pods.

New Role spec
- apiGroups: [""]
- resources: ["pods"]
- verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

- kubectl auth can-i list pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- The nginx Pod in web is running and healthy.
- The Service web/nginx existed but had a wrong selector: app=web.
- The Deployment labels Pods as app=nginx, so the Service matched 0 Pods, resulting in no endpoints and no traffic.
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress were blocking access.

Fix applied
- Updated the Service selector to match the Pod label app=nginx.
- Result: Endpoints are now populated for the Service (e.g., 10.244.0.30:80), so traffic to svc/web/nginx on port 80 now reaches the Pod.

How to verify
- From inside the cluster:
  - kubectl -n web get svc nginx,endpoints nginx
  - kubectl -n web run tmp --rm -it --image=busybox:1.36 --restart=Never -- wget -qO- http://nginx
- From your machine via port-forward:
  - kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80
  - curl http://localhost:8080

If you need external access
- Expose via an Ingress (you already have ingress-nginx installed) or change the Service to type NodePort/LoadBalancer. Tell me your desired hostname or exposure method and I can set it up.

taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- Your web-app pods were Pending due to a node selector constraint. Events showed: “didn't match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
- The Deployment had spec.template.spec.nodeSelector: { environment: production-gpu }, but the cluster has only one node without that label. So the scheduler couldn’t place the pods.
- Because no pods were backing the Service, web-app-service had no endpoints, causing connection refused to web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local.

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from the Deployment to allow scheduling on available nodes.
  - Deployment: webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment
  - Change: deleted spec.template.spec.nodeSelector: { environment: production-gpu }

Current status
- Pods:
  - web-app-deployment-69c75d9dcb-lhw54 — Running
  - web-app-deployment-69c75d9dcb-ffvbc — Running
- Service endpoints are now populated for web-app-service on port 80 and point to both pods.

What to do next
- Verify the frontend is no longer seeing connection refused.
- If you intended to restrict these pods to specific nodes, either:
  - Label the appropriate nodes and then re-add the selector (example: kubectl label node <node-name> environment=production-gpu), or
  - Use a correct selector that matches existing node labels.
- Optional hardening:
  - Pin the container image to a specific version instead of nginx:latest.
  - Add readiness/liveness probes to the Deployment.

If you want, I can re-apply a targeted nodeSelector once the correct node labels are in place, or switch to node affinity with fallbacks.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-app
  namespace: hpa-test
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 3
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 50

- Pod: communication-pod
  - Containers (in spec.containers):
    1) web-server: nginx:1.25-alpine
       - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
       - readinessProbe HTTP GET / on port 80 (pod becomes Ready when web server serves traffic)

    2) logger: busybox:1.36
       - command: sh -c "touch /var/log/nginx/access.log; tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"
       - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
  - Volume: logs-volume as emptyDir shared by both containers

Current status: Pod is Running and Ready.

Done.

What I changed:
- Enabled volume expansion on the StorageClass standard (allowVolumeExpansion: true).
- Resized the PersistentVolumeClaim resize-pv/storage-pvc to request 3Gi.
- Updated the bound PersistentVolume’s capacity to 3Gi.

Current state:
- PVC spec.requests.storage: 3Gi
- PV capacity: 3Gi
- Pod storage-pod is Running and continues to use the PVC.

Note:
- The local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path) doesn’t run an external resizer, so the PVC status.capacity field may still show 1Gi even though the PV and request are 3Gi. With local-path (a hostPath directory), there’s no real block device to grow; capacity isn’t enforced, and usable space is that of the node filesystem. If you need strict quota/enforcement, consider a CSI storage class that supports expansion with an external-resizer.

Note:
- The local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path) doesn’t run an external resizer, so the PVC status.capacity field may still show 1Gi even though the PV and request are 3Gi. With local-path (a hostPath directory), there’s no real block device to grow; capacity isn’t enforced, and usable space is that of the node filesystem. If you need strict quota/enforcement, consider a CSI storage class that supports expansion with an external-resizer.

taskOutput:
All set. I created and applied a complete configuration that meets your requirements:

What’s configured
- Namespaces:
  - dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- RBAC and ServiceAccounts:
  - ServiceAccounts: alice-sa (dev-alice), bob-sa (dev-bob), charlie-sa (dev-charlie)
  - Full access for each developer (User and their ServiceAccount) in their own namespace via a namespace-scoped Role and RoleBinding
  - Read-only access (get, list, watch) for each developer (User and ServiceAccount) to dev-shared
  - Only cluster admins can manage staging and prod (RoleBindings to system:masters → ClusterRole admin). No developer permissions granted there
- ResourceQuotas:
  - dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie: 2 CPU, 4Gi memory, 10 pods, 5 services
  - dev-shared: 4 CPU, 8Gi memory, 20 pods, 10 services
  - staging/prod: 8 CPU, 16Gi memory, 50 pods, 20 services
- NetworkPolicies (assumes a CNI that enforces NetworkPolicy):
  - Default deny ingress and egress in all non-system namespaces (dev-*, dev-shared, staging, prod)
  - Allow DNS egress (UDP/TCP 53) to CoreDNS (kube-system, k8s-app=kube-dns) from every non-system namespace
  - Dev namespaces:
    - Allow pod-to-pod within same namespace (ingress + egress)
    - Allow egress to dev-shared only
    - Block all other cross-namespace traffic (including to other developers and to staging/prod)
  - dev-shared:
    - Allow ingress from all dev namespaces (env=dev), including itself
    - Allow egress within dev-shared
  - staging/prod:
    - Fully isolated from dev namespaces (default deny in place; only DNS egress allowed)
- Principle of least privilege:
  - Developers have only what’s required: full control in their own namespace, read-only to dev-shared, and no access to staging/prod

Verification tips
- RBAC checks (examples):
  - kubectl auth can-i create deploy -n dev-alice --as alice
  - kubectl auth can-i get pods -n dev-shared --as alice
  - kubectl auth can-i list pods -n staging --as alice  (should be no)
- Network checks (from a test pod in dev-alice):
  - Same namespace allowed: curl http://<service or pod IP in dev-alice>
  - To dev-shared allowed: curl http://<service in dev-shared>
  - To dev-bob or staging/prod blocked
  - DNS works: nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local

Notes
- If your cluster labels for CoreDNS differ from k8s-app=kube-dns, update the allow-dns-egress NetworkPolicy selector accordingly.
- If developers will use their ServiceAccounts externally, you can mint projected tokens (K8s 1.24+) for those SAs as needed.

arguments.resource:
# ServiceAccounts for developers in their namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie

---
# RBAC: Full access for each developer (User) and their ServiceAccount in their own namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
  namespace: dev-alice
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["*"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-user-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: alice
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-sa-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
  namespace: dev-bob
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["*"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-user-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: bob
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-sa-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
  namespace: dev-charlie
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["*"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-user-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: charlie
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin-sa-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# RBAC: Read-only access to dev-shared for all developers (Users and their SAs)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: shared-readonly
  namespace: dev-shared
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: shared-readonly-users
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: alice
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
- kind: User
  name: bob
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
- kind: User
  name: charlie
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: shared-readonly
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: shared-readonly-serviceaccounts
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: shared-readonly
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# Resource Quotas
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"

---
# Network Policies
# Default deny all ingress and egress; allow DNS egress across all non-system namespaces
# dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          k8s-app: kube-dns
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          environment: dev
          shared: "true"

arguments.resource:
# Explicit RBAC bindings so only cluster admins manage staging and prod
# Bind the built-in ClusterRole admin (or cluster-admin) to the system:masters group in staging and prod
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: staging-admins
  namespace: staging
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: system:masters
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: prod-admins
  namespace: prod
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: system:masters
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

All set. I created and applied a complete configuration that meets your requirements:

What’s configured
- Namespaces:
  - dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- RBAC and ServiceAccounts:
  - ServiceAccounts: alice-sa (dev-alice), bob-sa (dev-bob), charlie-sa (dev-charlie)
  - Full access for each developer (User and their ServiceAccount) in their own namespace via a namespace-scoped Role and RoleBinding
  - Read-only access (get, list, watch) for each developer (User and ServiceAccount) to dev-shared
  - Only cluster admins can manage staging and prod (RoleBindings to system:masters → ClusterRole admin). No developer permissions granted there
- ResourceQuotas:
  - dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie: 2 CPU, 4Gi memory, 10 pods, 5 services
  - dev-shared: 4 CPU, 8Gi memory, 20 pods, 10 services
  - staging/prod: 8 CPU, 16Gi memory, 50 pods, 20 services
- NetworkPolicies (assumes a CNI that enforces NetworkPolicy):
  - Default deny ingress and egress in all non-system namespaces (dev-*, dev-shared, staging, prod)
  - Allow DNS egress (UDP/TCP 53) to CoreDNS (kube-system, k8s-app=kube-dns) from every non-system namespace
  - Dev namespaces:
    - Allow pod-to-pod within same namespace (ingress + egress)
    - Allow egress to dev-shared only
    - Block all other cross-namespace traffic (including to other developers and to staging/prod)
  - dev-shared:
    - Allow ingress from all dev namespaces (env=dev), including itself
    - Allow egress within dev-shared
  - staging/prod:
    - Fully isolated from dev namespaces (default deny in place; only DNS egress allowed)
- Principle of least privilege:
  - Developers have only what’s required: full control in their own namespace, read-only to dev-shared, and no access to staging/prod

Verification tips
- RBAC checks (examples):
  - kubectl auth can-i create deploy -n dev-alice --as alice
  - kubectl auth can-i get pods -n dev-shared --as alice
  - kubectl auth can-i list pods -n staging --as alice  (should be no)
- Network checks (from a test pod in dev-alice):
  - Same namespace allowed: curl http://<service or pod IP in dev-alice>
  - To dev-shared allowed: curl http://<service in dev-shared>
  - To dev-bob or staging/prod blocked
  - DNS works: nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local

Notes
- If your cluster labels for CoreDNS differ from k8s-app=kube-dns, update the allow-dns-egress NetworkPolicy selector accordingly.
- If developers will use their ServiceAccounts externally, you can mint projected tokens (K8s 1.24+) for those SAs as needed.
